**Plasma** (from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language") [πλάσμα](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CF%80%CE%BB%CE%AC%CF%83%CE%BC%CE%B1#Ancient_Greek "wikt:πλάσμα") *(*plásma*)* 'moldable substance')[[1]](#cite_note-πλάσμα-1) is a [state of matter](/wiki/State_of_matter "State of matter") that results from a [gaseous](/wiki/Gas "Gas") state having undergone some degree of [ionisation](/wiki/Ionisation "Ionisation"). It thus consists of a significant portion of [charged particles](/wiki/Charged_particle "Charged particle") ([ions](/wiki/Ion "Ion") and/or [electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron")). While rarely encountered on Earth, it is estimated that 99.9% of all [ordinary matter](/wiki/Ordinary_matter "Ordinary matter") in the [universe](/wiki/Universe "Universe") is plasma.[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Stars](/wiki/Star "Star") are almost pure balls of plasma, and plasma dominates the rarefied [intracluster medium](/wiki/Intracluster_medium "Intracluster medium") and [intergalactic medium](/wiki/Outer_space#Intergalactic_space "Outer space").[[3]](#cite_note-Itptma2013a-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Piel2010-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Phillips1995-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Aschwanden2004-6)
Plasma can be artificially generated, for example, by heating a neutral gas or subjecting it to a strong [electromagnetic field](/wiki/Electromagnetic_field "Electromagnetic field").[[7]](#cite_note-BoPA2015-7)

The presence of [charged particles](/wiki/Charged_particle "Charged particle") makes plasma [electrically conductive](/wiki/Electrically_conductive "Electrically conductive"), with the dynamics of individual particles and macroscopic plasma motion governed by collective electromagnetic fields and very sensitive to externally applied fields.[[8]](#cite_note-Itpd2012b-8) The response of plasma to electromagnetic fields is used in many modern devices and technologies, such as [plasma televisions](/wiki/Plasma_display "Plasma display") or [plasma etching](/wiki/Plasma_etching "Plasma etching").[[9]](#cite_note-Itptma2013-9)

Depending on temperature and density, a certain number of neutral particles may also be present, in which case plasma is called [partially ionized](/wiki/Partially_ionized "Partially ionized"). [Neon signs](/wiki/Neon_sign "Neon sign") and [lightning](/wiki/Lightning "Lightning") are examples of partially ionized plasmas.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Unlike the [phase transitions](/wiki/Phase_transition "Phase transition") between the other three states of matter, the transition to plasma is not well defined and is a matter of interpretation and context.[[11]](#cite_note-Itpd2012-11) Whether a given degree of ionization suffices to call a substance "plasma" depends on the specific phenomenon being considered.

## Early history

Plasma was first identified in laboratory by [Sir William Crookes](/wiki/Sir_William_Crookes "Sir William Crookes"). Crookes presented a [lecture](/wiki/Lecture "Lecture") on what he called "radiant matter" to the [British Association for the Advancement of Science](/wiki/British_Association_for_the_Advancement_of_Science "British Association for the Advancement of Science"), in Sheffield, on Friday, 22 August 1879.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Systematic studies of plasma began with the research of [Irving Langmuir](/wiki/Irving_Langmuir "Irving Langmuir") and his colleagues in the 1920s. Langmuir also introduced the term "plasma" as a description of ionized gas in 1928:[[13]](#cite_note-langmuir1928-13)

Except near the electrodes, where there are *sheaths* containing very few electrons, the ionized gas contains ions and electrons in about equal numbers so that the resultant space charge is very small. We shall use the name *plasma* to describe this region containing balanced charges of ions and electrons.

[Lewi Tonks](/wiki/Lewi_Tonks "Lewi Tonks") and Harold Mott-Smith, both of whom worked with Langmuir in the 1920s, recall that Langmuir first used the term by analogy with the [blood plasma](/wiki/Blood_plasma "Blood plasma").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) Mott-Smith recalls, in particular, that the transport of electrons from thermionic filaments reminded Langmuir of "the way blood plasma carries red and white corpuscles and germs."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Definitions

### The fourth state of matter

Plasma is called the *fourth [state of matter](/wiki/State_of_matter "State of matter")* after [solid](/wiki/Solid "Solid"), [liquid](/wiki/Liquid "Liquid"), and [gas](/wiki/Gas "Gas").[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) It is a state of matter in which an [ionized](/wiki/Ionized "Ionized") substance becomes highly [electrically conductive](/wiki/Electrical_resistivity_and_conductivity "Electrical resistivity and conductivity") to the point that long-range [electric and magnetic fields](/wiki/Electromagnetism "Electromagnetism") dominate its behaviour.[[20]](#cite_note-Chen,_Francis_F._2016_2–3-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Freidberg,_Jeffrey_P._2008_121-21)

Plasma is typically an electrically quasineutral medium of unbound positive and negative [particles](/wiki/Particle "Particle") (i.e., the overall charge of a plasma is roughly zero). Although these particles are unbound, they are not "free" in the sense of not experiencing forces. Moving charged particles generate [electric currents](/wiki/Electric_current "Electric current"), and any movement of a [charged](/wiki/Charged_particle "Charged particle") plasma particle affects and is affected by the [fields](/wiki/Electric_field "Electric field") created by the other charges. In turn, this governs collective behaviour with many degrees of variation.[[22]](#cite_note-Sturrock-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Plasma is distinct from the other states of matter. In particular, describing a low-density plasma as merely an "ionized gas" is wrong and misleading, even though it is similar to the gas phase in that both assume no definite shape or volume. The following table summarizes some principal differences:

| State  Property | Gas | Plasma |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Interactions | **Short-range**: Two-particle (binary) [collisions](/wiki/Collision "Collision") are the rule. | **Long-range**: Collective motion of particles is ubiquitous in plasma, resulting in various [waves](/wiki/Waves_in_plasma "Waves in plasma") and other types of collective phenomena. |
| Electrical conductivity | **Very low**: Gases are excellent [insulators](/wiki/Insulator_(electricity) "Insulator (electricity)") up to electric field strengths of tens of kilovolts per centimetre.[[24]](#cite_note-24) | **Very high**: For many purposes, the conductivity of a plasma may be treated as infinite. |
| Independently acting species | **One**: All gas particles behave in a similar way, largely influenced by collisions with one another and by [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity"). | **Two or more**: [Electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron") and [ions](/wiki/Ion "Ion") possess different [charges](/wiki/Electric_charge "Electric charge") and vastly different masses, so that they behave differently in many circumstances, with various types of plasma-specific waves and [instabilities](/wiki/Instability "Instability") emerging as a result. |

### Ideal plasma

Three factors define an ideal plasma:[[25]](#cite_note-Hazeltine-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Non-neutral plasma

The strength and range of the electric force and the good conductivity of plasmas usually ensure that the densities of positive and negative charges in any sizeable region are equal ("quasineutrality"). A plasma with a significant excess of charge density, or, in the extreme case, is composed of a single species, is called a [non-neutral plasma](/wiki/Non-neutral_plasmas "Non-neutral plasmas"). In such a plasma, electric fields play a dominant role. Examples are charged [particle beams](/wiki/Particle_beam "Particle beam"), an electron cloud in a [Penning trap](/wiki/Penning_trap "Penning trap") and positron plasmas.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Dusty plasma

A [dusty plasma](/wiki/Dusty_plasma "Dusty plasma") contains tiny charged particles of dust (typically found in space). The dust particles acquire high charges and interact with each other. A plasma that contains larger particles is called grain plasma. Under laboratory conditions, dusty plasmas are also called *complex plasmas*.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Temperature

Plasma temperature, commonly measured in [kelvin](/wiki/Kelvin "Kelvin") or [electronvolts](/wiki/Electronvolt "Electronvolt"), is a measure of the thermal kinetic energy per particle. High temperatures are usually needed to sustain ionization, which is a defining feature of a plasma. The degree of plasma ionization is determined by the [electron temperature](/wiki/Electron_temperature "Electron temperature") relative to the [ionization energy](/wiki/Ionization_energy "Ionization energy") (and more weakly by the density). In [thermal equilibrium](/wiki/Thermal_equilibrium "Thermal equilibrium"), the relationship is given by the [Saha equation](/wiki/Saha_equation "Saha equation"). At low temperatures, ions and electrons tend to recombine into bound states—atoms[[34]](#cite_note-Nicholson-34)—and the plasma will eventually become a gas.

In most cases, the electrons and heavy plasma particles (ions and neutral atoms) separately have a relatively well-defined temperature; that is, their energy [distribution function](/wiki/Distribution_function_(physics) "Distribution function (physics)") is close to a [Maxwellian](/wiki/Maxwell%E2%80%93Boltzmann_distribution "Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution") even in the presence of strong [electric](/wiki/Electric_field "Electric field") or [magnetic](/wiki/Magnetic_field "Magnetic field") fields. However, because of the large difference in mass between electrons and ions, their temperatures may be different, sometimes significantly so. This is especially common in weakly ionized technological plasmas, where the ions are often near the [ambient temperature](/wiki/Ambient_temperature "Ambient temperature") while electrons reach thousands of kelvin.[[35]](#cite_note-35) The opposite case is the [z-pinch](/wiki/Z-pinch "Z-pinch") plasma where the ion temperature may exceed that of electrons.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Plasma potential

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Bliksem_in_Assen.jpg/250px-Bliksem_in_Assen.jpg)

Since plasmas are very good [electrical conductors](/wiki/Electrical_conductor "Electrical conductor"), electric potentials play an important role.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] The average potential in the space between charged particles, independent of how it can be measured, is called the "plasma potential", or the "space potential". If an electrode is inserted into a plasma, its potential will generally lie considerably below the plasma potential due to what is termed a [Debye sheath](/wiki/Debye_sheath "Debye sheath"). The good electrical conductivity of plasmas makes their electric fields very small. This results in the important concept of "quasineutrality", which says the density of negative charges is approximately equal to the density of positive charges over large volumes of the plasma (

In the special case that *[double layers](/wiki/Double_layer_(plasma) "Double layer (plasma)")* are formed, the charge separation can extend some tens of Debye lengths.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

# Plasma Physics

As the name suggests, plasma physics concerns the physical properties of plasmas.

With increasing temperature, all materials are transformed successively from the solid, to liquid and then gaseous state. If the temperature is increased even more, a plasma is formed. Plasma is thus also described as the “fourth aggregate state of matter”: the gas atoms split into their constituent components – electrons and nuclei. Everyday examples of plasmas include plasma columns in neon tubes, electric sparks and the plasma filament in a lightning flash.

The properties of plasma are very different to those of a normal gas. For example, a plasma is electrically conductive; its motion can be influenced by electric and magnetic fields.

The fusion devices of the Max Planck Institute for Plasma Physics (IPP) in Garching (ASDEX Upgrade) and Greifswald (Wendelstein 7-X) exploit this particular property of plasma: they confine the hot plasma in a “magnetic field cage”.

## Fusion as an energy source

The [Max Planck Institute for Plasma Physics](https://www.ipp.mpg.de/en) (IPP) uses these devices to investigate the principles of a fusion power plant that would harness energy from the fusion of light atomic nuclei – just like the sun.

The aim here is to add to the range of efficient energy sources that could replace coal, oil and gas in the future: nuclear fusion is a third option along with nuclear fission in nuclear power plants and renewable energies, like wind and solar power.

The fusion of the hydrogen isotopes deuterium and tritium is the easiest form of fusion that can be achieved on Earth. The process generates a helium nucleus and a neutron is also released along with large volumes of usable energy. With fusion, one gram of combustible fuel could generate 90,000 kilowatt hours of energy in a power plant, which is equivalent to the combustion heat of eleven tonnes of coal.   
   
 Fusion fuels are inexpensive and evenly distributed throughout the world. Almost inexhaustible quantities of deuterium are available in seawater. Tritium – a radioactive gas with a short half-life of 12.3 years – only occurs in trace quantities in nature. However, it can be produced within the power plant from lithium, which is also available in abundance. Given that a fusion power plant could also offer favourable environmental and safety features, fusion could make a sustainable contribution to the world’s future energy supply.

# plasma

Our editors will review what you’ve submitted and determine whether to revise the article.

## News •

**plasma**, in [physics](https://www.britannica.com/science/physics-science), an electrically conducting medium in which there are roughly equal numbers of positively and negatively charged [particles](https://www.britannica.com/science/particle-physics), produced when the atoms in a gas become ionized. It is sometimes referred to as the fourth state of [matter](https://www.britannica.com/science/matter), distinct from the [solid](https://www.britannica.com/science/solid-state-of-matter), [liquid](https://www.britannica.com/science/liquid-state-of-matter), and [gaseous](https://www.britannica.com/science/gas-state-of-matter) states.

The negative [charge](https://www.britannica.com/science/electric-charge) is usually carried by [electrons](https://www.britannica.com/science/electron), each of which has one unit of negative charge. The positive charge is typically carried by atoms or molecules that are missing those same electrons. In some rare but interesting cases, electrons missing from one type of [atom](https://www.britannica.com/science/atom) or [molecule](https://www.britannica.com/science/molecule) become attached to another component, resulting in a plasma containing both positive and negative [ions](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/ions). The most extreme case of this type occurs when small but macroscopic dust particles become charged in a state referred to as a [dusty plasma](https://www.britannica.com/science/dusty-plasma). The uniqueness of the plasma state is due to the importance of electric and magnetic forces that act on a plasma in addition to such forces as [gravity](https://www.britannica.com/science/gravity-physics) that affect all forms of matter. Since these electromagnetic forces can act at large distances, a plasma will act collectively much like a [fluid](https://www.britannica.com/science/fluid-physics) even when the particles seldom collide with one another.

Nearly all the visible matter in the [universe](https://www.britannica.com/science/universe) exists in the plasma state, occurring predominantly in this form in the [Sun](https://www.britannica.com/place/Sun) and stars and in interplanetary and interstellar space. [Auroras](https://www.britannica.com/science/aurora-atmospheric-phenomenon), [lightning](https://www.britannica.com/science/lightning-meteorology), and [welding arcs](https://www.britannica.com/technology/arc-welding) are also plasmas; plasmas exist in neon and fluorescent tubes, in the [crystal structure](https://www.britannica.com/science/crystal) of metallic solids, and in many other phenomena and objects. The [Earth](https://www.britannica.com/place/Earth) itself is immersed in a [tenuous](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/tenuous) plasma called the [solar wind](https://www.britannica.com/science/solar-wind) and is surrounded by a dense plasma called the ionosphere.

A plasma may be produced in the laboratory by heating a gas to an extremely high [temperature](https://www.britannica.com/science/temperature), which causes such vigorous collisions between its atoms and molecules that electrons are ripped free, yielding the requisite electrons and ions. A similar process occurs inside stars. In space the dominant plasma formation process is [photoionization](https://www.britannica.com/science/photo-ionization), wherein photons from sunlight or starlight are absorbed by an existing gas, causing electrons to be emitted. Since the Sun and stars shine continuously, virtually all the matter becomes ionized in such cases, and the plasma is said to be fully ionized. This need not be the case, however, for a plasma may be only partially ionized. A completely ionized hydrogen plasma, consisting solely of electrons and protons (hydrogen nuclei), is the most elementary plasma.

## The development of [plasma physics](https://www.britannica.com/science/plasma-physics)

The modern concept of the plasma state is of recent origin, dating back only to the early 1950s. Its history is interwoven with many [disciplines](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/disciplines). Three basic fields of study made unique early contributions to the development of plasma physics as a discipline: electric discharges, [magnetohydrodynamics](https://www.britannica.com/science/magnetohydrodynamics) (in which a conducting fluid such as mercury is studied), and kinetic theory.

Interest in electric-discharge phenomena may be traced back to the beginning of the 18th century, with three English physicists—Michael Faraday in the 1830s and Joseph John Thomson and John Sealy Edward Townsend at the turn of the 19th century—laying the foundations of the present understanding of the phenomena. [Irving Langmuir](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Irving-Langmuir) introduced the term plasma in 1923 while investigating electric discharges. In 1929 he and Lewi Tonks, another physicist working in the [United States](https://www.britannica.com/place/United-States), used the term to designate those regions of a discharge in which certain periodic variations of the negatively charged electrons could occur. They called these oscillations [plasma oscillations](https://www.britannica.com/science/plasma-oscillation), their behaviour suggesting that of a jellylike substance. Not until 1952, however, when two other American physicists, [David Bohm](https://www.britannica.com/biography/David-Bohm) and David Pines, first considered the [collective](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/collective) behaviour of electrons in metals as distinct from that in ionized gases, was the general applicability of the concept of a plasma fully appreciated.

The collective behaviour of charged particles in magnetic fields and the concept of a conducting fluid are [implicit](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/implicit) in magnetohydrodynamic studies, the foundations of which were laid in the early and middle 1800s by Faraday and [André-Marie Ampère](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Andre-Marie-Ampere) of [France](https://www.britannica.com/place/France). Not until the 1930s, however, when new solar and geophysical phenomena were being discovered, were many of the basic problems of the mutual interaction between ionized gases and magnetic fields considered. In 1942 [Hannes Alfvén](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hannes-Alfven), a Swedish physicist, introduced the concept of magnetohydrodynamic waves. This contribution, along with his further studies of space plasmas, led to Alfvén’s receipt of the [Nobel Prize](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Nobel-Prize) for Physics in 1970.

![](https://cdn.britannica.com/marketing/BlueThistle.webp)
![How scientists use lasers to learn about the universe](https://cdn.britannica.com/86/179686-138-C038EAA6/PHELIX-laser-GSI-Helmholtz-Centre-for-Heavy.jpg?w=800&h=450&c=crop)

These two separate approaches—the study of electric [discharges](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/discharges) and the study of the behaviour of conducting fluids in magnetic fields—were unified by the introduction of the [kinetic theory](https://www.britannica.com/science/kinetic-theory) of the plasma state. This theory states that plasma, like gas, consists of particles in random [motion](https://www.britannica.com/science/motion-mechanics), whose interactions can be through long-range electromagnetic forces as well as via collisions. In 1905 the Dutch physicist [Hendrik Antoon Lorentz](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hendrik-Antoon-Lorentz) applied the kinetic equation for atoms (the formulation by the Austrian physicist Ludwig Eduard Boltzmann) to the behaviour of electrons in metals. Various physicists and mathematicians in the 1930s and ’40s further developed the plasma kinetic theory to a high degree of sophistication. Since the early 1950s interest has increasingly focused on the plasma state itself. Space exploration, the development of electronic devices, a growing awareness of the importance of magnetic fields in astrophysical phenomena, and the quest for controlled thermonuclear (nuclear fusion) power reactors all have [stimulated](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/stimulated) such interest. Many problems remain unsolved in space plasma physics research, owing to the complexity of the phenomena. For example, descriptions of the solar wind must include not only equations dealing with the effects of gravity, temperature, and [pressure](https://www.britannica.com/science/pressure) as needed in [atmospheric science](https://www.britannica.com/science/atmospheric-science) but also the equations of the Scottish physicist [James Clerk Maxwell](https://www.britannica.com/biography/James-Clerk-Maxwell), which are needed to describe the [electromagnetic field](https://www.britannica.com/science/electromagnetic-field).

# About Plasmas and Fusion

## What is Plasma?

Plasma is a state of matter along with solids, liquids and gases. When a neutral gas is heated such that some of the electrons are freed from the atoms or molecules, it changes state and becomes a plasma. It consists of a partially-ionized gas, containing ions, electrons, and neutral atoms.

![Aurora Alaska](/sites/g/files/toruqf286/files/styles/freeform_1440w/public/2021-08/Aurora_Borealis_Alaska_4.jpg?itok=hMtijMKw)
![Lightening](/sites/g/files/toruqf286/files/styles/freeform_1440w/public/2021-08/cr%20desert-lightning-1408110525Yi1.png?itok=jHIGg-_C)

## So what does that mean?

The free negative electrons and positive ions in a plasma allow electric current to flow through it. In a plasma, electrons are freed from their atoms, allowing current to flow and enabling the plasma to react to both electric and magnetic fields. This is a very useful characteristic as you’ll see later... In fact, one of the few naturally occurring plasmas found here on Earth is lightning!

What are some examples of plasmas?

Lightning!

Fluorescent light bulbs contain mercury plasma.

Stars, such as the sun are hot balls of plasma.

Aurora Borealis and Aurora Australis

Fusion reactors, like NSTX-U, use plasma to fuse atoms to make energy.

Plasma displays use small cells of plasma to illuminate images.

## What is fusion?

Fusion, the power that drives the sun and stars, combines light elements in the form of plasma — the hot, charged state of matter composed of free electrons and atomic nuclei — that generates massive amounts of energy. Scientists are seeking to replicate fusion on Earth for a virtually inexhaustible supply of power to generate electricity.

Fusion occurs when the nuclei of light atoms like hydrogen (one proton and one neutron) fuse together and create a new nucleus, releasing energy.

The sun’s core is a natural fusion reactor. Before the sun formed, it was a cloud of gas mostly made up of hydrogen and some helium. At some point, the cloud became so massive that gravity caused it to collapse in on itself. At that point, numerous collisions in the sun’s core freed electrons from atoms, creating plasma and leaving behind nuclei called ions. When contact between ions became so frequent that they got close enough to fuse, the ball of gas ignited and became a star.

On Earth, researchers are trying to build fusion reactors of their own. They seek to maximize the number of ions in a small region and the amount of time that they stay close together. To do that, fusion reactors heat plasmas to temperatures much hotter than the core of the sun — over 100 million degrees Celsius. Strong magnetic fields or high-powered lasers then confine the plasma into small controllable regions where fusion can happen.

PPPL and many labs, universities and private companies around the world are studying the process of creating and optimizing these fusion reactors and developing the technology to convert the fusion energy into usable electricity. Doughnut-shaped facilities known as tokamaks,  such as PPPL’s National Spherical Torus Experiment-Upgrade (NSTX-U), pump most of the extracted energy into fast neutrons. Energy from these neutrons can be collected in a blanket consisting of a molten mixture of lithium and lead that surrounds the plasma. Excess heat in this blanket can then be used to make steam to drive turbines and generate electricity.

![fusion](/sites/g/files/toruqf286/files/styles/freeform_1440w/public/2021-06/Deuterium-tritium_fusion_svg.png?itok=XthxP0BA)
![fusion power](/sites/g/files/toruqf286/files/styles/freeform_1440w/public/2021-06/Image_2.jpeg?itok=49Oud_u7)

## What is Fusion?

For more than 70 years, the U.S. Department of Energy’s Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory has been a leader in the science and engineering behind the development of fusion — a clean, safe, and virtually limitless source of energy.

This video features Stefan Gerhardt, senior managing research physicist of the National Spherical Tokamak Experiment (NSTX), and Jessica Guttenfelder, heating project engineer, both from PPPL.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Plasma is an ionized state of matter consisting of free electrons and ions, characterized by high conductivity and responsiveness to electromagnetic fields.
Plasma forms when a gas is heated to high temperatures, causing atoms to ionize and freeing electrons, resulting in a mixture of charged particles.
Plasma is electrically conductive, exhibits collective behavior under electromagnetic influence, and can exist in fully or partially ionized states, unlike other matter states.

enriched_points.point_title: Comparison with Other States of Matter
enriched_points.expanded_content: Unlike solids, liquids, and gases, plasma is dominated by electromagnetic forces, resulting in distinct properties such as high conductivity and collective particle dynamics.
Natural plasmas include stars, lightning, auroras, and solar wind, showcasing plasma's prevalence in both cosmic and terrestrial phenomena.
